turns.content: I just got this error when running my tests with axum_test: 'cannot infer type for the trait object'. I tried calling routes without binding a port as suggested in some docs but it failed. Can you help?

It looks like you're misunderstanding how to invoke your handler inside an integration test. When using tower::ServiceExt, you need to actually send a request through the service rather than just calling the handler directly without context. The error happens because axum_test expects a proper Service implementation that can handle requests.
Oh, I see. But I thought if I don't bind a port I could just call my handler function and assert JSON responses? That seemed simpler in other frameworks.

That's a common misconception when coming from frameworks like actix-web where you might bind and listen on a port more easily. In axum, even for testing without binding a network port, you must pass the request through tower::ServiceExt to simulate HTTP behavior correctly. The handler itself isn't an endpoint until wrapped in a service that accepts requests.
Right, so I should be using ServiceExt? But how do I actually make the call without needing a real server running?

You use axum_test::TestService to wrap your router and then invoke it with an http request like reqwest or hyper. This avoids binding ports entirely while still letting you assert JSON responses by mocking the incoming request and checking outgoing response bytes.
Got it, so I need to build a test service from my axum Router, not just call the handler directly? And that's why the type inference failed?

Exactly. By wrapping your router in a TestService you provide tower::ServiceExt implementation which lets you construct requests and responses programmatically without needing network sockets or port bindings.
Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to test my handler but the router still needs a real TCP socket and port binding. Why can't I just call the service trait directly?

That is actually how it was designed in early versions before we adopted tower::ServiceExt for testing. The idea is that your application should not require a bound port to function, so you can use axum_test or tower::ServiceExt to invoke routes without starting an actual TCP listener. Think of it like calling a vending machine's dispenser mechanism directly rather than standing at the front with cash in hand.

But if I'm just testing the logic, why do I still need to set up an entire request/response cycle? I feel like this is forcing me to manage too much infrastructure when I only want to verify my handler returns valid JSON.

You are right that we don't need a real port, but the abstraction layer still needs to simulate the full request/response cycle because your handler operates on requests and responses. The tower::ServiceExt trait allows you to treat your application as a service that accepts inputs and produces outputs without needing an actual socket connection.
Okay so I still need to build the entire request object manually? That seems like more work than I expected for just testing if my JSON serialization is correct.

Exactly. You do need to construct a full Request object that mimics what a real client would send, including headers and body content. The benefit is that once you have the service set up with tower::ServiceExt, calling it on your test request bypasses all network stack overhead while still giving you access to the exact bytes returned by your handler.
I'm onboarding to a new project using axum and I want to test my handlers without actually binding a port. How do I call routes in tests?

That's exactly the right approach for integration testing. You can use the tower::ServiceExt trait which allows you to invoke a service directly without needing a server listening on a socket. Instead of starting an actual axum server with the TcpListener, you just call your handler like a normal function and pass in test data. This way you're calling the route directly through the tower layer rather than going through network I/O.
So I don't need to start a TCP listener at all? What's the benefit of avoiding that complexity?

You are absolutely right, you skip all the socket setup entirely. The benefit is that your tests become deterministic and fast because they bypass network latency completely. Think of it like testing a gear mechanism by turning the crank directly inside the engine bay versus connecting an external motor to spin the shaft. By calling the route directly through tower::ServiceExt, you're simulating incoming requests in memory without waiting for actual packets to arrive at a port.
I see. And how do I verify the JSON response in this testing scenario?

You assert the response by parsing the output into serde_json::Value or using the reqwest crate to read the body as bytes and then deserialize it into your expected struct. This lets you check that your handler produces correct JSON without needing a browser or curl tool. The test framework will run these assertions against the mock service output.
That makes sense. Can I still use axum::Router in my tests?

Yes, you build the router normally with axum::Router and then extract the inner service using the tower method. You don't need to convert it to a full server just for testing purposes. The router itself is just a composition of your handlers and middleware, so extracting that service layer lets you invoke them exactly how they would be called in production but without binding anything.
Got it. I'll start writing tests using tower::ServiceExt to mock the incoming requests and assert on JSON output.

That sounds like a solid plan. Just remember that while you're avoiding port binding, your tests should still cover edge cases like malformed JSON or missing headers to ensure robustness before deployment.

I'm struggling to test my new Axum service because I can't seem to start the server just to make requests. My code uses tower::ServiceExt but every time I try to bind a port, it blocks my tests and slows down development significantly.

That is frustrating when you are learning Rust because it feels like you have to choose between blocking on startup or writing verbose manual TCP stacks. The solution you need is actually simpler than you think; instead of starting the server, you can use axum_test which allows you to invoke routes directly without binding a port at all.

I tried importing axum_test but I don't know how to call my handler function. My current code looks like this: let response = server.get("/test").await?; println!("Got {}", serde_json::to_string(&response).unwrap());

Your approach is close but you are missing a crucial step in the chain. The function you called needs to be wrapped inside an axum_test::TestRequest so that it knows which route to hit and what headers to send along with your test payload.
Ah okay, but I'm confused about the exact syntax. Do I need to create a separate TestApp instance or can I just inject this into my existing app structure?

You do not need a separate application; you simply wrap your existing service with axum_test::TestService before invoking the handler. This lets you assert on the response body directly without needing to parse raw bytes or manage connections manually.
Got it, but I still have a hard time understanding how to verify specific JSON fields in my test assertions.

Once you invoke the handler with your TestRequest, the result is a Response object that contains the status code and headers. You can then extract the body using axum::extract::Body or use serde_json to parse it into a struct so you can assert specific fields like the user_id field.
I think I finally get how to do this. One last question though, what happens if my handler returns an error like Result<T, E>?

If your handler returns a result type that includes errors, you must handle the Err case before passing it into axum_test::TestService. Otherwise, the test will panic because it expects a valid response body.
